edges.description: ATP7B copper trafficking normally supports hepatocyte copper movement into the bile excretion pathway.
The same ATP7B secretory-pathway trafficking defect prevents copper incorporation into apoceruloplasmin.
Loss of biliary copper excretion leads to progressive copper retention in hepatocytes.
Failure to load copper into apoceruloplasmin leads to low circulating ceruloplasmin.
Copper overload in hepatocytes drives oxidative stress and cell death.
Overflow of copper from the liver enters the circulation and deposits in extrahepatic organs.
Retained hepatic copper becomes measurably increased on liver biopsy.
Copper deposition in the basal ganglia and other brain regions causes neuronal injury.
Overflow of hepatocellular copper raises the circulating free copper fraction.
Increased circulating free copper drives elevated urinary copper excretion.
Copper deposition in Descemet membrane produces Kayser-Fleischer corneal rings.
Copper deposition in the kidneys contributes to renal tubular injury and dysfunction.
Cardiac copper deposition contributes to myocardial injury and cardiomyopathy.
Copper-mediated cardiac injury can progress to clinical heart failure.
intermediate_mechanisms: Copper-related cardiomyopathy and cardiac remodeling.

edges.description: Copper-mediated cardiac involvement increases the risk of clinically significant arrhythmias.
Extrahepatic copper toxicity contributes to reduced bone mineral density.
intermediate_mechanisms: Renal tubular dysfunction, altered mineral balance, and reduced bone mineral density.

edges.description: Chronic extrahepatic copper toxicity contributes to pathologic bone loss in a subset of patients.
intermediate_mechanisms: Renal tubular dysfunction, altered mineral balance, and reduced bone mineral density.

edges.description: Musculoskeletal copper deposition and bone disease contribute to arthralgia.
intermediate_mechanisms: Wilson disease osteoarticular involvement and bone-density abnormalities.

edges.description: Copper deposition in ocular tissues can produce the rare sunflower cataract phenotype.
Systemic copper disease and chronic hepatic dysfunction can disrupt reproductive cycling through incompletely resolved endocrine intermediates.
Copper-related skeletal disease and low bone mineral density can manifest with bone pain.
intermediate_mechanisms: Osteopenia, osteoporosis, osteomalacia, and renal tubular mineral dysregulation.

edges.description: Systemic copper-related musculoskeletal involvement includes arthritic manifestations in a subset of patients.
intermediate_mechanisms: Musculoskeletal copper toxicity, arthropathy, and low bone-density complications.

edges.description: Musculoskeletal involvement can present with inflammatory or arthropathic joint swelling.
intermediate_mechanisms: Wilson disease arthropathy and related skeletal inflammation.

edges.description: Chronic skeletal involvement with osteopenia or osteoporosis increases vulnerability to pathologic fracture.
intermediate_mechanisms: Osteopenia and osteoporosis.

edges.description: Injured hepatocytes activate pro-fibrotic stellate-cell responses in the liver.
intermediate_mechanisms: Copper-triggered TGF-beta/Smad and NF-kappaB signaling.

edges.description: Copper-driven hepatic inflammation can drive neuroinflammation and neurological deficits through a liver-brain crosstalk axis, partly independent of direct brain copper deposition.
intermediate_mechanisms: Hepatic inflammation and circulating inflammatory cytokines signaling to the CNS (liver-brain axis).
CNS microglial activation and inflammatory cytokine elevation.

edges.description: Hepatocellular oxidative injury and inflammation manifest clinically as hepatitis.
Hepatocyte swelling and inflammatory liver injury can produce hepatomegaly.
intermediate_mechanisms: Hepatic inflammation, cell swelling, and organ enlargement.

edges.description: Acute hepatocellular dysfunction impairs bilirubin handling and can produce jaundice.
intermediate_mechanisms: Hepatocellular dysfunction and impaired bilirubin handling.

edges.description: Severe hepatocyte death can precipitate fulminant hepatic failure.
Abrupt copper release from injured liver can trigger oxidant hemolysis.
intermediate_mechanisms: Abrupt hepatic copper release and oxidant red-cell injury.

edges.description: Acute hepatic decompensation can present with vomiting as part of the hepatic illness syndrome.
intermediate_mechanisms: Acute hepatic illness and decompensation.

edges.description: Proposed copper-dependent cell death pathways may amplify hepatocyte injury.
intermediate_mechanisms: Copper overload, mitochondrial stress, and copper-dependent cell-death signaling.

edges.description: Secondary iron deposition may superimpose ferroptotic injury in a subset of patients.
intermediate_mechanisms: Secondary hepatic iron accumulation and lipid-peroxidation stress.

edges.description: Copper-mediated hepatic injury commonly produces steatosis on liver biopsy.
Acute hepatocellular injury can present with an acute hepatitis phenotype.
Hepatocyte injury releases hepatic enzymes, producing elevated circulating transaminases.
Hepatic dysfunction and cholestatic intermediates can contribute to pruritus in Wilson disease.
intermediate_mechanisms: Hepatic dysfunction and impaired bile-acid handling.

edges.description: Hepatic inflammation and decompensation can present with abdominal pain.
intermediate_mechanisms: Hepatic inflammation, hepatomegaly, and acute liver decompensation.

edges.description: Chronic hepatic disease and systemic illness can impair growth and weight gain.
intermediate_mechanisms: Chronic liver disease, reduced intake, and systemic inflammatory illness.

edges.description: Chronic hepatic disease and systemic illness can produce catabolic weight loss.
intermediate_mechanisms: Chronic liver disease, reduced intake, and systemic inflammatory illness.

edges.description: Hepatic decompensation can contribute to anemia through hemolytic and advanced-liver-disease intermediates.
intermediate_mechanisms: Coombs-negative hemolysis and advanced liver disease.

edges.description: Activated stellate cells and myofibroblasts drive excess extracellular matrix deposition.
Progressive matrix deposition and architectural distortion manifest clinically as cirrhosis.
Cirrhotic architectural distortion can lead to portal hypertension and fluid accumulation as ascites.
intermediate_mechanisms: Cirrhosis and portal hypertension.

edges.description: Fibrotic progression to cirrhosis can produce portal-hypertension-associated splenomegaly.
intermediate_mechanisms: Cirrhosis, portal hypertension, and congestive splenic enlargement.

edges.description: Cirrhosis and portal hypertension can cause hypersplenism-associated thrombocytopenia.
intermediate_mechanisms: Cirrhosis, portal hypertension, splenomegaly, and hypersplenism.

edges.description: Advanced hepatic fibrosis can contribute to bruising through synthetic dysfunction and thrombocytopenia.
intermediate_mechanisms: Cirrhosis, impaired coagulation factor synthesis, and thrombocytopenia.

edges.description: Basal ganglia and cerebellar circuit injury contributes to tremor.
Striatal injury disrupts motor circuit output and contributes to dystonia.
Basal ganglia dysfunction can manifest clinically as parkinsonism.
Extrapyramidal circuit dysfunction contributes to bradykinesia.
Extrapyramidal circuit dysfunction contributes to rigidity.
Basal ganglia injury can produce choreiform hyperkinesia.
Motor network injury affecting bulbar and extrapyramidal function contributes to dysarthria.
Cerebellar and brainstem involvement contributes to ataxia.
Bulbar motor dysfunction contributes to dysphagia.
Bulbar dysfunction and impaired swallowing contribute to drooling.
intermediate_mechanisms: Bulbar dysfunction and impaired swallowing.

edges.description: Copper-mediated brain injury can contribute to neuropsychiatric symptoms including anxiety.
intermediate_mechanisms: Neuropsychiatric circuit dysfunction.

edges.description: Neuropsychiatric brain involvement contributes to depressive symptoms.
intermediate_mechanisms: Neuropsychiatric circuit dysfunction.

edges.description: Severe neuropsychiatric involvement can manifest as psychosis.
intermediate_mechanisms: Neuropsychiatric circuit dysfunction.

edges.description: Fronto-striatal and related network dysfunction can manifest as personality change.
intermediate_mechanisms: Fronto-striatal and related neuropsychiatric circuit dysfunction.

edges.description: Copper-related brain injury can cause cognitive dysfunction; the intellectual-disability HPO mapping is retained as a broader cognitive phenotype.
intermediate_mechanisms: Cognitive dysfunction from copper-related neural injury.

edges.description: Structural and metabolic brain involvement can lower seizure threshold in a subset of patients.
intermediate_mechanisms: Copper-related brain lesions and neuroinflammation.

edges.description: Basal ganglia, cerebellar, and brainstem involvement can disrupt gait.
Motor-circuit involvement can present clinically as clumsiness.
Bulbar dysfunction and impaired swallowing can produce excessive salivation.
intermediate_mechanisms: Bulbar motor dysfunction and dysphagia.

edges.description: Neurologic motor involvement and systemic muscle effects can present as proximal lower-limb weakness.
intermediate_mechanisms: Extrapyramidal motor dysfunction and Wilson disease muscle involvement.

edges.description: Brainstem involvement on MRI reflects focal structural injury within neurologic Wilson disease.
Neuropsychiatric involvement can manifest with irritability and aggression.
intermediate_mechanisms: Psychiatric manifestations of copper-related brain involvement.

edges.description: Low circulating ceruloplasmin reports failed ATP7B-dependent copper incorporation into apoceruloplasmin.
Higher non-ceruloplasmin-bound copper reflects the circulating free-copper burden after hepatic copper handling fails.
Increased urinary copper excretion reflects excess circulating/free copper available for renal excretion.
Elevated liver copper content directly reports hepatic copper accumulation.

evidence.reference_title: Wilson disease.
evidence.snippet: The diagnosis of WD is commonly made on the basis of typical clinical and laboratory findings, including low serum ceruloplasmin, increased urinary copper excretion, and increased hepatic copper content.

evidence.reference_title: Non-ceruloplasmin copper and urinary copper in clinically stable Wilson disease: Alignment with recommended targets.
evidence.snippet: Only 14/69 (20%) fulfilled both the NCC-Sp and UCE targets.

readouts.interpretation: Increased urinary copper excretion reflects excess circulating/free copper available for renal excretion.

nodes.description: Investigational adeno-associated virus (AAV8) gene therapy delivering a functional (often truncated, e.g. ΔC4) ATP7B coding sequence to hepatocytes to restore copper export. Preclinical Wilson disease mouse studies show normalized ceruloplasmin, reduced hepatic and urinary copper, and reversed liver histopathology, supporting clinical translation. Not yet an approved therapy.

evidence.reference_title: Evaluation of efficacy and safety of AAV8-ΔC4ATP7B gene therapy in a mutant mouse model of Wilson's disease.
evidence.snippet: Treated Atp7b KI/KI mice showed normalization of serum ceruloplasmin, reduced hepatic Cu, decreased urinary Cu, and reversed liver histopathology

evidence.reference_title: Therapeutic implications of impaired nuclear receptor function and dysregulated metabolism in Wilson's disease.
evidence.snippet: Inactivating mutations of the copper-transporting P-type ATPase, ATP7B, result in Wilson's disease, an autosomal recessive disorder

evidence.reference_title: Wilson disease.
evidence.snippet: Thus far, more than 200 mutations of the WD gene have been detected, causing impairment of ATP7B function and, ultimately, copper accumulation.

nodes.description: Copper-dependent cell death has been proposed in Wilson disease, involving binding of copper to lipoylated enzymes in the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle and leading to proteotoxic stress and mitochondrial dysfunction. The strongest Wilson disease-specific evidence currently comes from ATP7B knockout mouse and copper-treated hepatocyte models rather than human tissue.

evidence.reference_title: Wilson Disease: Copper-Mediated Cuproptosis, Iron-Related Ferroptosis, and Clinical Highlights, with Comprehensive and Critical Analysis Update.
evidence.snippet: Many interesting disease details were published on the mechanistic steps, such as the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and cuproptosis causing a copper dependent cell death

evidence.reference_title: The pathogenesis of liver fibrosis in Wilson's disease: hepatocyte injury and regulation mediated by copper metabolism dysregulation.
evidence.snippet: Moreover, abnormal lipoylation of the copper-dependent proteins metal-binding domain of ferredoxin 1 and dihydrolipoamide transacetylase causes mitochondrial protein oligomer buildup and tricarboxylic acid cycle dysfunction, reinforcing an "oxidative damage-inflammation-fibrosis" vicious cycle.

evidence.reference_title: Copper induces cell death by targeting lipoylated TCA cycle proteins.
evidence.snippet: copper-dependent, regulated cell death is distinct from known death mechanisms and is dependent on mitochondrial respiration.

evidence.reference_title: Uncovering the Critical Role of Cuproptosis in Wilson Disease: Insights Into Potential Therapeutic Targets.
evidence.snippet: Cuproptosis markers (FDX1, DLST, DLAT, LIAS) were upregulated in both liver tissue

evidence.reference_title: Uncovering the Critical Role of Cuproptosis in Wilson Disease: Insights Into Potential Therapeutic Targets.
evidence.snippet: Copper exposure decreased cell viability and increased LDH release, exacerbated by Elesclomol and alleviated by Tetrathiomolybdate.

nodes.description: Copper chelator, first-line therapy, promotes urinary copper excretion.

evidence.reference_title: Wilson Disease: Copper-Mediated Cuproptosis, Iron-Related Ferroptosis, and Clinical Highlights, with Comprehensive and Critical Analysis Update.
evidence.snippet: Patients with Wilson disease are well-treated first-line with copper chelators like D-penicillamine that facilitate the removal of circulating copper bound to albumin and increase in urinary copper excretion. Early chelation therapy improves prognosis.

evidence.reference_title: Neurological worsening in Wilson disease - clinical classification and outcome.
evidence.snippet: NW was observed with D-penicillamine, trientine and zinc therapy and was reversible in 15/30 (50%) with early NW and in 29/36 (81%) with late NW.

nodes.description: Iron-related cell death pathway involving phospholipid peroxidation. Secondary hepatic iron overload has been documented in a subset of Wilson disease patients, raising the possibility of ferroptotic injury alongside copper-mediated toxicity. This remains an emerging modifier mechanism rather than a universal explanation for Wilson disease liver injury.

evidence.reference_title: Iron and Copper Liver Concentrations in Wilson Disease.
evidence.snippet: 13 (8%) patients aged 13 or older had a hepatic iron index >1.0 which may indicate secondary iron overload.

evidence.reference_title: Wilson Disease: Copper-Mediated Cuproptosis, Iron-Related Ferroptosis, and Clinical Highlights, with Comprehensive and Critical Analysis Update.
evidence.snippet: In the liver of patients with Wilson disease, also, increased iron deposits were found that may lead to iron-related ferroptosis responsible for phospholipid peroxidation within membranes of subcellular organelles

evidence.reference_title: Quercetin alleviates liver injury in Wilson's disease by inhibiting ferroptosis.
evidence.snippet: Mechanistically, QUE was confirmed to directly bind to ACSL4 and inhibit the ACSL4/LPCAT3/ALOX15 signaling pathway, thereby blocking ferroptosis.

evidence.reference_title: Relationship between oxidative stress and antioxidant systems in the liver of patients with Wilson disease: hepatic manifestation in Wilson disease as a consequence of augmented oxidative stress.
evidence.snippet: Decreased ratios of reduced glutathione (GSH) to oxidized glutathione (GSSG) and increased thiobarbituric acid reactive substance (TBARS), a lipid peroxidation product, were found in every affected patient, especially in group II and III patients.

evidence.snippet: Hemolytic anemia in WD occurs in up to 17% of patients at some point during their illness.

evidence.reference_title: Hemolytic anemia in Wilson disease: clinical findings and biochemical mechanisms.
evidence.snippet: Two patients with Wilson disease who presented with severe hemolytic anemia are described.

readouts.interpretation: Elevated liver copper content directly reports hepatic copper accumulation.

nodes.description: Impaired biliary excretion causes progressive copper retention in hepatocytes. When hepatic storage capacity is exceeded, copper is released into the circulation.

evidence.reference_title: Wilson disease.
evidence.snippet: Wilson disease (WD) is an autosomal recessive inherited disorder of copper metabolism, resulting in pathological accumulation of copper in many organs and tissues.

evidence.reference_title: Wilson Disease: Copper-Mediated Cuproptosis, Iron-Related Ferroptosis, and Clinical Highlights, with Comprehensive and Critical Analysis Update.
evidence.snippet: Released from the liver cells due to limited storage capacity, the toxic copper enters the circulation and arrives at other organs, causing local accumulation and cell injury.

evidence.reference_title: Wilson disease - liver pathology.
evidence.snippet: Most liver biopsy specimens show moderate to severe steatosis, variable degree of portal and/or lobular inflammation, and fibrosis eventually progressing to cirrhosis.

evidence.reference_title: Wilson disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001397 | Hepatic steatosis | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Copper-injured liver tissue activates pro-fibrotic signaling, including TGF-beta and NF-kappaB pathways, which stimulate hepatic stellate cells to transdifferentiate toward collagen-secreting myofibroblasts.

biological_processes: transforming growth factor beta receptor signaling pathway

evidence.reference_title: The pathogenesis of liver fibrosis in Wilson's disease: hepatocyte injury and regulation mediated by copper metabolism dysregulation.
evidence.snippet: Copper ions also activate signaling pathways such as the TGF-β1/Smad and NF-κB pathways, which stimulate hepatic stellate cells and promote their transdifferentiation into collagen-secreting myofibroblasts

evidence.reference_title: Wilson disease - liver pathology.
evidence.snippet: Most liver biopsy specimens show moderate to severe steatosis, variable degree of portal and/or lobular inflammation, and fibrosis eventually progressing to cirrhosis.

evidence.reference_title: Wilson disease - liver pathology.
evidence.snippet: Wilson disease may be microscopically misinterpreted as many other liver diseases, including viral or autoimmune hepatitis, alcoholic/nonalcoholic steatohepatitis, toxic liver injury, cryptogenic cirrhosis

evidence.reference_title: Wilson disease (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0012115 | Hepatitis | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Copper accumulation in hepatocytes causes oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, impaired bioenergetics, and cell death. Severe injury can manifest as hepatitis, jaundice, hepatomegaly, and acute liver failure. Copper overload also activates autophagy and mitophagy-based mitochondrial quality control responses, while cuproptosis and ferroptosis may amplify injury in model systems and susceptible hepatic contexts.

evidence.reference_title: Wilson Disease: Copper-Mediated Cuproptosis, Iron-Related Ferroptosis, and Clinical Highlights, with Comprehensive and Critical Analysis Update.
evidence.snippet: Excess Cu2+ primarily leads to the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), as evidenced by early experimental studies exemplified with the detection of hydroxyl radical formation

evidence.reference_title: Pathological mitochondrial copper overload in livers of Wilson's disease patients and related animal models.
evidence.snippet: On the contrary, however, when copper is massively deposited in mitochondria, severe structural and respiratory impairments are observed upon disease progression. This provokes reactive oxygen species and consequently causes the mitochondrial membranes to disintegrate, which triggers hepatocyte death.

evidence.reference_title: Wilson disease - liver pathology.
evidence.snippet: Most liver biopsy specimens show moderate to severe steatosis, variable degree of portal and/or lobular inflammation, and fibrosis eventually progressing to cirrhosis.

evidence.reference_title: Activation of Autophagy, Observed in Liver Tissues From Patients With Wilson Disease and From ATP7B-Deficient Animals, Protects Hepatocytes From Copper-Induced Apoptosis.
evidence.snippet: ATP7B-deficient hepatocytes, such as in those in patients with WD, activate autophagy in response to copper overload to prevent copper-induced apoptosis.

evidence.reference_title: Activation of Autophagy, Observed in Liver Tissues From Patients With Wilson Disease and From ATP7B-Deficient Animals, Protects Hepatocytes From Copper-Induced Apoptosis.
evidence.snippet: Hepatocytes in liver tissues from patients with WD and from Atp7b-/- mice and rats (but not controls) had multiple autophagosomes.

evidence.reference_title: Exposure to copper induces mitochondria-mediated apoptosis by inhibiting mitophagy and the PINK1/parkin pathway in chicken (Gallus gallus) livers.
evidence.snippet: In general, the results reveal that Cu exposure can cause mitophagy through the PINK1/Parkin pathway in chicken livers, and that mitophagy might attenuate Cu-induced mitochondrial apoptosis.

evidence.reference_title: Mitochondrial dysfunction in Wilson disease: a systematic review and meta-analysis across human and animal models.
evidence.snippet: Mitochondrial dysfunction arises early and worsens with progression, highlighting it as a central pathogenic feature and therapeutic target.

evidence.reference_title: Liver injury in Wilson's disease: An immunohistochemical study.
evidence.snippet: Negligible COX4-1 and low COX2 expression in liver specimens may serve as markers of inner mitochondrial membrane injury in treated patients with WD and early stages of liver fibrosis.

evidence.reference_title: Wilson's disease: an analysis of 28 Brazilian children.
evidence.snippet: three had hepatomegaly associated with neurological disorders

nodes.description: ATP7B mutations impair copper excretion into bile. ATP7B normally resides in the trans-Golgi network and traffics to the bile canalicular membrane when intracellular copper is elevated. Loss of ATP7B function prevents this copper export pathway.

evidence.reference_title: Wilson Disease: Copper-Mediated Cuproptosis, Iron-Related Ferroptosis, and Clinical Highlights, with Comprehensive and Critical Analysis Update.
evidence.snippet: Copper accumulation in the liver is due to impaired biliary excretion of copper caused by the inheritable malfunctioning or missing ATP7B protein.

evidence.reference_title: Wilson disease.
evidence.snippet: ATP7B is the gene product of the WD gene located on chromosome 13 and resides in hepatocytes in the trans-Golgi network, transporting copper into the secretory pathway for incorporation into apoceruloplasmin and excretion into the bile.

evidence.reference_title: ATP7B copper-regulated traffic and association with the tight junctions: copper excretion into the bile.
evidence.snippet: Copper excess provokes a massive download of the ATP7B retained in the trans-Golgi network into the bile canalicular membrane.

nodes.description: ATP7B normally loads copper into apoceruloplasmin in the trans-Golgi network. Loss of ATP7B function results in secretion of copper-free apoceruloplasmin, which is rapidly degraded, leading to the characteristic low serum ceruloplasmin levels.

evidence.reference_title: Wilson disease.
evidence.snippet: ATP7B is the gene product of the WD gene located on chromosome 13 and resides in hepatocytes in the trans-Golgi network, transporting copper into the secretory pathway for incorporation into apoceruloplasmin and excretion into the bile.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000952 | Jaundice | Very frequent (99-80%)

evidence.reference_title: Clinical presentation, diagnosis and long-term outcome of Wilson's disease: a cohort study.
evidence.snippet: Diagnostic criteria for non-caeruloplasmin-bound serum copper, serum caeruloplasmin, 24-h urinary copper excretion, liver copper content, presence of Kayser-Fleischer rings and histological signs of chronic liver damage were reached in 86.6%, 88.2%, 87.1%, 92.7%, 66.3% and 73% of patients, respectively.

evidence.reference_title: Wilson Disease: Copper-Mediated Cuproptosis, Iron-Related Ferroptosis, and Clinical Highlights, with Comprehensive and Critical Analysis Update.
evidence.snippet: copper injures not only the liver, but also the brain, kidneys, eyes, heart, muscles, and bones, explaining the multifaceted clinical features of Wilson disease. Among these are depression, psychosis, dysarthria, ataxia, writing problems, dysphagia, renal tubular dysfunction, Kayser-Fleischer corneal rings

nodes.description: Curative for hepatic Wilson disease, reverses copper metabolism defect.

evidence.reference_title: Pediatric Wilson Disease Presenting as Acute Liver Failure: An Individual Patient Data Meta-analysis.
evidence.snippet: Of the total 256 subjects, 87% underwent LT, 11% achieved spontaneous recovery and 2% died before LT.

evidence.reference_title: Wilson Disease: Copper-Mediated Cuproptosis, Iron-Related Ferroptosis, and Clinical Highlights, with Comprehensive and Critical Analysis Update.
evidence.snippet: Liver transplantation is an option viewed as ultima ratio in end-stage liver disease with untreatable complications or acute liver failure. Liver transplantation finally may thus be a life-saving approach and curative treatment of the disease by replacing the hepatic gene mutation.

nodes.description: Adjunctive dietary measure; liver and shellfish are the foods most consistently recommended for avoidance. Per GeneReviews, alcohol consumption should be strongly discouraged in patients with biochemical liver function or transaminase abnormalities.

evidence.reference_title: Low Copper Diet-A Therapeutic Option for Wilson Disease?
evidence.snippet: In summary, consistent adherence to drug therapy is more important than a strict diet. Only two foods should be consistently avoided: Liver and Shellfish.

evidence.reference_title: Wilson Disease (GeneReviews).
evidence.snippet: In case of biochemical abnormalities in liver function tests or transaminases, alcohol consumption is strongly discouraged

nodes.description: Copper deposition is thought to drive brain cellular damage with characteristic striatal/basal ganglia lesions. Longitudinal atrophy data support active neurodegeneration, while reactive astrogliosis and microglial activation with increased CNS inflammatory cytokines indicate neuroinflammation contributing to movement and psychiatric symptoms.

evidence.reference_title: Wilson disease: brain pathology.
evidence.snippet: In Wilson disease (WD), brain cellular damage is thought to be due to copper deposition. Striatal lesions are the most characteristic lesions found in the brain of patients with neurologic symptoms, as emphasized in the initial reports of S.A.K. Wilson.

evidence.reference_title: Neurologic impairment in Wilson disease.
evidence.snippet: Most severe neuropathologic abnormalities, including tissue rarefaction, reactive astrogliosis, myelin palor, and presence of iron-laden macrophages, are typically present in the putamen while other basal ganglia, thalami, and brainstem are usually less affected.

evidence.reference_title: Aberrant copper metabolism and hepatic inflammation cause neurological manifestations in a mouse model of Wilson's disease.
evidence.snippet: At 3-5 months of age, these mice started to display neurological deficits in motor coordination and cognitive dysfunction, accompanied by increased expression of inflammatory cytokines in the central nervous system. Microglia in the striatum and cortex exhibit significant activation, shorter processes, and decreased branch points.

evidence.reference_title: Brain Atrophy Is Substantially Accelerated in Neurological Wilson's Disease: A Longitudinal Study.
evidence.snippet: The annualized atrophy rate was significantly greater in patients with the neurological presentation than in other patients (P = 0.001).

evidence.reference_title: Topographical metal burden correlates with brain atrophy and clinical severity in Wilson's disease.
evidence.snippet: magnetic susceptibility values were increased in WD patients compared with controls, including caudate nucleus, putamen, globus pallidus, and substantia nigra (all p < 0.01).

evidence.reference_title: Clinical presentation, diagnosis and long-term outcome of Wilson's disease: a cohort study.
evidence.snippet: Diagnostic criteria for non-caeruloplasmin-bound serum copper, serum caeruloplasmin, 24-h urinary copper excretion, liver copper content, presence of Kayser-Fleischer rings and histological signs of chronic liver damage were reached in 86.6%, 88.2%, 87.1%, 92.7%, 66.3% and 73% of patients, respectively.

evidence.reference_title: Non-ceruloplasmin copper and urinary copper in clinically stable Wilson disease: Alignment with recommended targets.
evidence.snippet: Chelator treatment of patients with Wilson disease (WD) is currently guided by measurements of non-ceruloplasmin-bound copper (NCC)

readouts.interpretation: Higher non-ceruloplasmin-bound copper reflects the circulating free-copper burden after hepatic copper handling fails.

evidence.reference_title: Osteoporosis and bone mineral density in patients with Wilson's disease: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
evidence.snippet: The pooled prevalencerates of osteopenia and osteoporosis in WD patients were 36.5%

evidence.reference_title: Renal Tubular Function, Bone Health and Body Composition in Wilson's Disease: A Cross-Sectional Study from India.
evidence.snippet: Patients with WD had significantly lower BMD as compared to control subjects (p < 0.05).

evidence.reference_title: Osteoporosis and bone mineral density in patients with Wilson's disease: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
evidence.snippet: The pooled prevalencerates of osteopenia and osteoporosis in WD patients were 36.5%

evidence.reference_title: Renal Tubular Function, Bone Health and Body Composition in Wilson's Disease: A Cross-Sectional Study from India.
evidence.snippet: Patients with WD had significantly lower BMD as compared to control subjects (p < 0.05).

evidence.snippet: most common neurological manifestation was dystonia, followed by tremor and parkinsonism.

evidence.reference_title: Wilson disease: neurologic features.
evidence.snippet: spectrum of neurologic manifestations that includes tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, dystonia, chorea, dysarthria, and dysphagia

evidence.reference_title: The psychiatric presentations of Wilson's disease.
evidence.snippet: Personality changes, particularly irritability and aggression, were most commonly described (45.9%), followed by depression (27%).

evidence.reference_title: The psychiatric presentations of Wilson's disease.
evidence.snippet: Cognitive changes, anxiety, psychosis, and catatonia, while less frequent, also occurred.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000709 | Psychosis | Occasional (29-5%)

evidence.snippet: Fifty-six percent (14/25) of patients with WD had renal tubular acidosis (RTA).

nodes.description: When hepatic copper storage capacity is exceeded, free copper enters the circulation and deposits in the brain, kidneys, eyes, heart, muscles, and bones, causing multi-organ injury.

evidence.reference_title: Wilson Disease: Copper-Mediated Cuproptosis, Iron-Related Ferroptosis, and Clinical Highlights, with Comprehensive and Critical Analysis Update.
evidence.snippet: Released from the liver cells due to limited storage capacity, the toxic copper enters the circulation and arrives at other organs, causing local accumulation and cell injury. This explains why copper injures not only the liver, but also the brain

evidence.reference_title: A study of oxidative stress, cytokines and glutamate in Wilson disease and their asymptomatic siblings.
evidence.snippet: Free copper in Wilson disease (WD) is toxic and may reduce antioxidant, increase oxidative stress marker and thereby cytokine release and excitotoxic injury, but there is paucity of studies in humans.

nodes.description: Investigational copper-binding agent (bis-choline tetrathiomolybdate, also known as tiomolibdate choline / ALXN1840) that markedly reduces intestinal copper uptake and lowers hepatic and brain copper exposure.

evidence.reference_title: Effects of tetrathiomolybdate on copper metabolism in healthy volunteers and in patients with Wilson disease.
evidence.snippet: TTM effectively inhibited most intestinal 64Cu uptake and retained 64Cu in the blood stream, limiting the exposure of organs like the liver and brain to 64Cu.

evidence.reference_title: Effect of tiomolibdate choline on copper balance in patients with Wilson disease: An open-label phase 2 trial.
evidence.snippet: TMC showed a statistically significant reduction in daily Cu balance from baseline over the entire study period (days 1-39), attributed to increased fecal Cu excretion.

nodes.description: Alternative copper chelator, better tolerated than penicillamine.

evidence.reference_title: Efficacy and Safety of Two Salts of Trientine in the Treatment of Wilson's Disease.
evidence.snippet: The two salts of trientine were effective in treating patients with WD.

evidence.reference_title: Three novel mutations in the ATP7B gene of unrelated Vietnamese patients with Wilson disease.
evidence.snippet: These patients had clinical features such as numbness of hands and feet, vomiting, insomnia, palsy, liver failure and Kayser-Fleischer (K-F) rings
